refashioned: Bullion deposits are most commonly made when the market price is somewhat below normal, and withdrawn when it rises. In Holland, the market price of bullion generally exceeds the mint price, for the same reason it did in England before the recent gold coin reform. The difference is typically between six and sixteen stivers per mark (eight ounces of silver at eleven parts fine and one part alloy). The bank price -- the credit given for deposits of well-known foreign silver coins such as Mexican dollars -- is twenty-two guilders per mark. The mint price is about twenty-three guilders, and the market price ranges from twenty-three guilders six stivers to twenty-three guilders sixteen stivers -- roughly two to three percent above the mint price.
elegant: Bullion is deposited when prices are low and withdrawn when they rise. In Holland, the market price of bullion typically exceeds the mint price by 2-3%. The bank credits Mexican dollars at 22 guilders per mark against a mint price of 23 guilders and a market price of 23 guilders 6-16 stivers.
summary: Bullion deposits flow into the bank when prices are low and flow out when prices rise. The bank credits silver at 22 guilders per mark, while the mint price is 23 guilders and the market price runs 2-3% higher still.
distilled: The bank's bullion deposit system functioned as a warehouse where merchants deposited when prices were low and withdrew when they rose, with the bank's credit price sitting below both mint and market prices.
notes: The bank as a bullion price-smoothing mechanism -- merchants arbitrage between bank price, mint price, and market price. Mexican dollars -- widely trusted silver coins of known fineness, used as a benchmark. Bank price (22) < mint price (23) < market price (23.06-23.16 guilders per mark). The spread between these prices is the economic space in which the receipt system operates. Cross-reference: 4.3.23 on the relationship between bank price, mint price, and market price for receipts.

refashioned:
The following are the prices at which the Bank of Amsterdam, as of September 1775, receives bullion and coin of different kinds:

Silver: Mexican dollars, 22 guilders per mark; French crowns, 22; English silver coin, 22; new Mexican dollars, 21 guilders 10 stivers; ducatoons, 3 guilders; rix-dollars, 2 guilders 8 stivers; bar silver containing eleven-twelfths fine silver, 21 guilders per mark, and proportionally down to one-quarter fine at 5 guilders. Fine bars, 28 guilders per mark.
elegant: As of September 1775, the Bank of Amsterdam's silver rates ranged from 22 guilders per mark for Mexican dollars and French crowns down to 5 guilders for one-quarter fine bar silver, with fine bars commanding 28 guilders per mark.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam published specific rates for receiving various silver coins and bars, with prices varying by fineness and coin type, from 5 guilders per mark for low-grade silver to 28 guilders for fine bars.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam's detailed schedule of silver deposit rates reflected the varying fineness and reliability of different coins and bullion.
notes: A primary source snapshot -- Smith records actual bank rates as of September 1775. Mexican dollars, French crowns, English silver -- the major international silver coins. Rate schedule reflects fineness: known-fineness coins get better rates than bars requiring assay. Fine bars (28 guilders) vs. standard Mexican dollars (22 guilders) -- the premium for higher purity. Cross-reference: 4.3.22 for gold rates; 4.3.23 on how these rates relate to market prices.

refashioned: Gold: Portuguese coin, 310 guilders per mark; guineas, 310; new Louis d'ors, 310; old Louis d'ors, 300; new ducats, 4 guilders 19 stivers 8 penningen per ducat. Bar or ingot gold is received in proportion to its fineness compared with the above foreign gold coins. For fine bars, the bank gives 340 per mark. In general, however, somewhat more is given for coins of known fineness than for gold and silver bars, whose fineness can only be established through the process of melting and assaying.
elegant: Gold rates at the Bank of Amsterdam: major coins (Portuguese, guineas, new Louis d'ors) at 310 guilders per mark; old Louis d'ors at 300; fine gold bars at 340. Coins of known fineness receive slightly better rates than bars requiring assay.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam's gold rates ranged from 300 guilders per mark for older coins to 340 for fine bars, with coins of known fineness receiving a premium over bars that required assaying.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam's gold deposit rates rewarded coins of known fineness over bars requiring assay, reflecting the cost and uncertainty of verification.
notes: Gold rate schedule complements the silver rates in 4.3.21. Major gold coins: Portuguese (moeda), English guineas, French Louis d'ors. Old vs. new Louis d'ors -- 300 vs. 310, reflecting wear. The assay premium: known-fineness coins > bars requiring verification. Fine gold bars at 340 -- the pure-metal benchmark. Cross-reference: 4.3.19 on why gold warehouse rent exceeds silver's.

refashioned: The relationships between the bank price, mint price, and market price of gold bullion are roughly the same as for silver. A person can generally sell his receipt for the difference between the mint price and the market price of bullion. A receipt for bullion is almost always worth something, and so it very seldom happens that anyone allows his receipt to expire or lets his bullion become the bank's property at the receiving price -- either by failing to withdraw before the six-month deadline or by neglecting to pay the quarter or half percent fee to obtain a new receipt for another six months. This does occasionally happen, however, and more frequently with gold than silver, because of the higher warehouse rent charged for the more precious metal.
elegant: Receipts are worth the spread between mint price and market price -- since bullion's market price usually exceeds the mint price, receipts almost always have value. Holders rarely let them expire, though it happens more often with gold due to the higher storage fees.
summary: Bullion receipts are valuable because they represent the spread between mint and market prices. Receipt holders almost never let them expire, though gold receipts lapse more often due to higher storage fees.
distilled: Bullion receipts derive their value from the spread between mint and market prices, and are almost always renewed -- though gold receipts lapse more often due to higher storage costs.
notes: Receipt valuation explained: receipt value = market price - mint price (approximately). Self-regulating system: since receipts have positive value, holders maintain them. Gold vs. silver lapse rates -- higher storage costs for gold make marginal deposits unprofitable to maintain. 'Falls to the bank' -- the bank's revenue from expired receipts (detailed in 4.3.26). Cross-reference: 4.3.24-4.3.25 on how receipts and bank credits circulate independently.

refashioned: The person who deposits bullion and obtains both a bank credit and a receipt uses the bank credit to pay his bills of exchange as they come due, and either sells or keeps his receipt depending on whether he expects the price of bullion to rise or fall. The receipt and the bank credit rarely stay together for long, and there is no need for them to. A person who has a receipt and wants to withdraw bullion always finds plenty of bank credits available to buy at the standard price. A person who has bank credits and wants bullion always finds receipts equally available.
elegant: Depositors use their bank credit to pay bills and trade their receipts based on bullion price expectations. Credits and receipts circulate independently -- those needing bullion can always buy bank credits, and those with bank credits can always buy receipts. The two instruments need not stay paired.
summary: Bank credits and bullion receipts circulate independently after deposit. Merchants use credits to pay bills and trade receipts based on bullion price expectations, with both instruments readily available in the market.
distilled: Bank credits and bullion receipts separate after deposit and circulate independently, each finding ready buyers in the market.
notes: The separation of bank credit from bullion receipt -- two distinct financial instruments from a single deposit. The receipt as a speculative instrument on bullion prices. Liquidity of both markets: credits and receipts are always available. This is essentially a description of how a derivatives market functions -- the receipt is an option on physical bullion. Cross-reference: 4.3.25 on the distinct roles of credit holders and receipt holders.

refashioned:
The owners of bank credits and the holders of receipts are two different classes of creditors against the bank. A receipt holder cannot withdraw the bullion it covers without returning to the bank a sum of bank money equal to the price at which the bullion was originally received. If he has no bank money of his own, he must buy it from those who do. A bank-money owner cannot withdraw bullion without presenting a receipt for the quantity he wants. If he has no receipt, he must buy one from those who do.

The receipt holder, when he buys bank money, is purchasing the right to withdraw bullion whose mint price is five percent above the bank price. The five percent agio he typically pays is therefore paid for a real, not an imaginary, value. The bank-money owner, when he buys a receipt, is purchasing the right to withdraw bullion whose market price typically runs two to three percent above the mint price. The price he pays for the receipt is likewise paid for real value. The receipt price and the bank-money price together make up the full value of the bullion.
elegant: Receipt holders and bank-money owners are distinct creditors. Withdrawing bullion requires both a receipt and equivalent bank money -- each must buy what the other holds. The receipt holder pays a 5% agio for bank money, gaining access to bullion worth 5% more than the bank price. The bank-money owner pays for a receipt, gaining access to bullion worth 2-3% more than the mint price. Together, receipt price and bank-money agio constitute the full bullion value.
summary: Withdrawing bullion requires both a receipt and bank money, creating two complementary markets. Receipt holders buy bank money at a 5% premium; bank-money owners buy receipts reflecting the 2-3% spread above mint price. Together these prices constitute the full market value of the bullion.
distilled: Bullion withdrawal requires combining a receipt with bank money, creating two complementary instruments whose combined price equals the full market value of the bullion.
notes: The two-key system: withdrawal requires both receipt AND bank money -- neither alone suffices. Each instrument represents a real, not nominal, value: the agio reflects the gap between bank and mint price; the receipt reflects the gap between mint and market price. A sophisticated financial architecture -- effectively a two-part derivative on physical bullion. Cross-reference: 4.3.26 on what happens when receipts for domestic coin become worthless.

refashioned:
For deposits of the country's own circulating currency, the bank also grants receipts and bank credits, but these receipts are frequently worthless and bring no price in the market. For ducatoons, which circulate at three guilders three stivers each, the bank gives a credit of only three guilders -- five percent below their current value -- and a receipt allowing withdrawal within six months upon paying one-quarter percent for storage.

This receipt typically has no market value. Three guilders in bank money generally sell for three guilders three stivers in the market -- the full value of the ducatoons if withdrawn. But withdrawal requires paying the quarter-percent storage fee, which would be a pure loss to the receipt holder. If the bank's agio were to fall to three percent, however, such receipts might have some value and sell for about one and three-quarters percent. Since the agio is generally around five percent, these receipts are usually allowed to expire.

Receipts for gold ducats expire even more frequently, because the higher warehouse rent of one-half percent must be paid before they can be withdrawn. The five percent the bank gains when deposits of coin or bullion are allowed to expire may be considered the warehouse rent for perpetual storage.
elegant: For domestic coin deposits, the bank gives credit at 5% below face value and issues receipts -- but these receipts are usually worthless. Since bank money already trades at the full face value of the deposited coins, paying a storage fee to withdraw them would be pure loss. Receipts expire, and the bank keeps the 5% discount as perpetual warehouse rent. Gold ducats expire even more often due to higher storage fees.
summary: Receipts for domestic coin deposits are usually worthless because bank money already trades at the coins' full face value, making withdrawal unprofitable after storage fees. When receipts expire, the bank retains the 5% discount as perpetual warehouse rent.
distilled: Domestic coin receipts are typically worthless because bank money already reflects their full value, so the 5% discount becomes the bank's perpetual warehouse rent when receipts expire.
notes: Asymmetry between bullion receipts (valuable) and domestic coin receipts (worthless). The arithmetic: bank credits domestic coin at 95% of face value; bank money trades at 100% of face value; withdrawal costs 0.25-0.5% storage fee -- so withdrawing is always a loss. The bank's revenue model: expired receipts yield a 5% perpetual rent on the underlying deposits. Edge case: if agio falls to 3%, receipts regain some value (receipts worth approximately agio - 5% credit discount). Cross-reference: 4.3.27 on the scale of expired receipts; 4.3.34 on total bank revenues.

refashioned:
The total sum of bank money represented by expired receipts must be very considerable. It must include the bank's entire original capital, which was deposited as coin and is generally believed to have remained there from the beginning, since nobody cared either to renew the receipt or to withdraw the deposit -- both options entailing a loss, for the reasons already explained.

But whatever its amount, this sum is believed to be very small relative to the total mass of bank money. For many years, the Bank of Amsterdam has been Europe's great bullion warehouse, and receipts for bullion very seldom expire. The far greater part of all bank money -- the credits on the bank's books -- is believed to have been created by bullion deposits that dealers are continually both making and withdrawing.
elegant: Expired receipts represent the bank's entire original coin capital, which no one ever bothered to withdraw. But this is believed to be small relative to total bank money, most of which now comes from the constant flow of bullion deposits and withdrawals by dealers -- the bank having become Europe's great bullion warehouse.
summary: The bank's original coin deposits, represented by expired receipts, remain unclaimed but are believed to be small relative to total bank money. Most bank credits now arise from the constant turnover of bullion deposits by Europe's bullion dealers.
distilled: The bank's original coin deposits remain permanently unclaimed, but most bank money now derives from the constant flow of bullion dealing.
notes: Two layers of bank money: (1) the original frozen coin deposits (receipts expired) and (2) active bullion deposits constantly circulating. The bank as Europe's central bullion warehouse -- a role Amsterdam maintained for over a century. The frozen original capital as a permanent endowment. 'Continually both making and withdrawing' -- the velocity of bullion transactions through the bank. Cross-reference: 4.3.28 on the mixing of expired and active bank money.

refashioned: No claim can be made on the bank except by presenting a receipt. The smaller mass of bank money for which receipts have expired is mixed and indistinguishable from the much larger mass for which receipts are still valid. Though there may be a considerable sum of bank money backed by no receipt, there is no specific portion of it that might not at any time be claimed by someone. The bank cannot owe the same money to two people. An owner of bank money who has no receipt cannot demand payment until he buys one. In ordinary, peaceful times, he can always find receipts to buy at the market price, which generally corresponds to the price at which the receipt entitles him to withdraw coin or bullion from the bank.
elegant: Bank money backed by expired receipts is indistinguishable from bank money backed by active receipts -- all bank money looks the same on the books. No withdrawal is possible without a receipt, so holders of 'orphaned' bank money must buy receipts on the open market. In normal times, receipts are always available at a price reflecting the underlying bullion value.
summary: Bank money from expired receipts is mixed with bank money from active receipts, making them indistinguishable. Since withdrawal requires a receipt, owners of receipt-less bank money must purchase receipts on the market, which are readily available in normal times.
distilled: Bank money from expired and active receipts is indistinguishable, and withdrawal always requires purchasing a receipt on the open market.
notes: The fungibility of bank money -- no distinction between 'orphaned' (no receipt) and 'paired' (receipt active) bank money. The bank's liability structure: cannot owe the same guilder to two parties. Market clearing: receipt prices adjust to balance supply and demand in normal conditions. Sets up the crisis scenario in 4.3.29 -- what happens when receipts are hoarded. Cross-reference: 4.3.25 on the two-key withdrawal system.

refashioned:
Things might be different during a public calamity -- an invasion, for example, such as the French invasion of 1672. If bank-money owners all rushed to withdraw their money to keep it in their own hands, the demand for receipts might drive their price to extraordinary heights. Receipt holders might form extravagant expectations and demand, instead of the usual two or three percent, half the bank money credited against the deposits their receipts covered. An invading enemy, understanding the bank's structure, might even buy up receipts to prevent the treasure from being carried away.

In such emergencies, the bank is supposed to break its ordinary rule of paying only receipt holders. Receipt holders without bank money would receive within two or three percent of their deposit's value. The bank would then pay the full credited value to bank-money owners who could not obtain receipts, while paying the remaining two or three percent to receipt holders without bank money -- that being the full value to which they could justly claim in such circumstances.
elegant: In a crisis -- like the French invasion of 1672 -- everyone would rush to withdraw, driving receipt prices to extortionate heights. Receipt holders might demand half the bank money for their receipts; an invading enemy might buy up receipts to trap the treasure. In such emergencies, the bank would break its normal rules: pay bank-money owners directly and settle with receipt holders at 2-3% -- their fair share given the circumstances.
summary: During a crisis like the 1672 French invasion, demand for receipts would spike to extreme levels. The bank would be expected to abandon its normal two-key withdrawal system, paying bank-money owners directly and settling with receipt holders at a reduced rate reflecting crisis conditions.
distilled: In a crisis, the bank's normal two-key withdrawal system would break down as receipt prices spike, forcing the bank to pay bank-money owners directly and settle with receipt holders at reduced rates.
notes: The crisis scenario -- stress-testing the bank's institutional design. The 1672 French invasion of the Netherlands as the historical reference case. Strategic vulnerability: an enemy could weaponize the receipt system by hoarding receipts. Emergency protocol: abandon the two-key system, pay bank-money owners directly. Receipt holders' residual claim reduced to 2-3% -- reflecting the separation of their real economic interest from the bank-money owners' interest. Cross-reference: 4.3.32 on the bank's actual performance during the 1672 crisis.

refashioned:
Even in ordinary, peaceful times, receipt holders have an interest in depressing the agio -- either to buy bank money (and thus the bullion their receipts would let them withdraw) more cheaply, or to sell their receipts at a higher price to bank-money owners who want to withdraw bullion. The price of a receipt generally equals the difference between the market price of bank money and the price of the coin or bullion the receipt covers. Bank-money owners, conversely, have an interest in raising the agio to sell their bank money dearer or buy receipts cheaper.

To prevent the speculative tricks these opposing interests might provoke, the bank recently resolved to sell bank money for currency at a five percent agio at all times, and to buy it back at four percent. As a result, the agio can never rise above five percent or fall below four percent, and the market price of bank money relative to common currency stays very close to the ratio of their intrinsic values. Before this resolution, the market price of bank money sometimes rose as high as nine percent agio and sometimes fell to par, depending on which group of speculators dominated the market.
elegant: Receipt holders want the agio low (to buy bank money cheaply); bank-money owners want it high (to sell dearly). To prevent speculative manipulation from these opposing interests, the bank now stands ready to sell bank money at 5% agio and buy at 4%, capping the range. Before this policy, the agio swung between 0% and 9% depending on which speculators dominated.
summary: Receipt holders and bank-money owners have opposing interests in the agio level. To prevent speculative manipulation, the bank adopted a standing offer to sell at 5% and buy at 4% agio, stabilizing the range that previously swung between 0% and 9%.
distilled: The bank stabilized the agio between 4-5% by standing ready to buy and sell bank money at those rates, preventing the speculative swings from 0-9% that opposing interests had caused.
notes: The bank as market maker -- standing bid and ask prices to stabilize the agio. A remarkably modern monetary policy tool: central bank intervention through standing facilities. The opposing interests: receipt holders (bears on agio) vs. bank-money owners (bulls on agio). Pre-intervention volatility: 0% to 9% -- destabilizing for commerce. The 4-5% band as a proto-currency corridor. Cross-reference: 4.3.25 on opposing incentives; modern central bank standing facilities.

refashioned:
The Bank of Amsterdam professes to lend out no part of what is deposited with it. For every guilder of credit on its books, it claims to keep the equivalent value in money or bullion in its vaults. That it keeps all the money or bullion for which valid receipts exist -- which it may at any time be called upon to produce, and which in practice is constantly flowing in and out -- can hardly be doubted.

But whether it does the same for that portion of its capital represented by long-expired receipts -- for which, in ordinary times, it cannot be called upon, and which will very likely remain with it forever, or at least as long as the United Provinces endure -- is perhaps more doubtful. In Amsterdam, however, no article of faith is more firmly established than this: that for every guilder circulating as bank money, there is a corresponding guilder in gold or silver in the bank's vaults.
elegant: The bank claims to hold full reserves -- a guilder of metal for every guilder of credit. For active receipts, this is surely true since deposits constantly flow in and out. But for the original capital behind long-expired receipts -- money that may never be claimed -- full reserves are more doubtful, though in Amsterdam no belief is more firmly held.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam claims full reserve backing for all bank money. While this is clearly true for active bullion deposits, it is less certain for the original capital behind expired receipts. Nevertheless, Amsterdammers hold this full-reserve claim as an article of faith.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam claims 100% reserve backing, which is clearly true for active deposits but more doubtful for permanently unclaimed original capital.
notes: The full-reserve claim -- and Smith's gentle skepticism. Two tiers of deposits: active (constantly cycling, verifiably backed) and dormant (original capital, never claimed). Smith's subtle doubt: 'may perhaps appear more uncertain' -- he suspects the bank may use dormant deposits. 'No point of faith is better established' -- the ironic religious language signals Smith's skepticism about whether faith matches reality. The question of whether the bank secretly lent out dormant funds was later confirmed -- the bank did lend to the Dutch East India Company. Cross-reference: 4.3.32 on the oversight mechanisms; historical confirmation that the bank was not fully reserved.

refashioned:
The city guarantees that this is so. The bank is directed by four reigning burgomasters who are replaced every year. Each new set of burgomasters inspects the treasure, compares it with the books, receives it under oath, and delivers it with the same solemn ceremony to their successors. In that sober and religious country, oaths are not yet treated lightly. Such a rotation of oversight seems in itself a sufficient safeguard against any practices that cannot be publicly acknowledged.

Amidst all the political upheavals that faction has caused in Amsterdam's government, the prevailing party has never once accused its predecessors of mismanaging the bank. No accusation could have damaged a disgraced party's reputation and fortune more deeply, and if such a charge could have been sustained, we may be certain it would have been made.

In 1672, when the French king was at Utrecht, the bank paid out so promptly as to leave no doubt about the fidelity with which it had kept its commitments. Some of the coins brought from the vaults appeared to have been scorched by the fire that struck the town hall shortly after the bank's founding. Those coins must therefore have lain undisturbed there from that time.
elegant: The city guarantees the bank's reserves. Four burgomasters, rotated yearly, inspect the treasure under oath -- a sufficient check in a country where oaths are sacred. No political faction has ever accused predecessors of bank mismanagement, though such an accusation would be devastating. In the 1672 crisis, the bank paid so promptly that its fidelity was beyond doubt -- some withdrawn coins still bore scorch marks from a fire shortly after the bank's founding decades earlier.
summary: Amsterdam's bank is overseen by annually rotating burgomasters who inspect reserves under oath. No political faction has ever accused predecessors of mismanagement. During the 1672 French invasion, the bank paid promptly, producing coins scorched by a fire from its early years -- proving they had lain undisturbed for decades.
distilled: Annual oversight rotations, the absence of any political accusation of mismanagement, and the 1672 crisis performance all supported confidence in the Bank of Amsterdam's full reserves.
notes: Three forms of assurance: (1) annual audits under oath by rotating officials, (2) political incentive -- opposition parties would exploit any irregularity, (3) the 1672 stress test. The scorched coins as physical proof -- an evocative historical detail. Ironic in hindsight: the bank was later discovered to have lent secretly to the Dutch East India Company and the city of Amsterdam, undermining its full-reserve claim. 'Sober and religious country' -- Dutch cultural norms as institutional safeguard. Cross-reference: 4.3.31 on Smith's gentle skepticism about full reserves.

refashioned: What the bank's total treasure amounts to has long been a matter of speculation. Only guesswork can be offered. It is generally estimated that about 2,000 people maintain accounts with the bank. Allowing them an average of 1,500 pounds sterling each -- a very generous estimate -- the total quantity of bank money, and therefore of treasure in the bank, would come to about three million pounds sterling, or roughly 33 million guilders at eleven guilders to the pound. This is a substantial sum, sufficient to support extensive commerce, but vastly below the extravagant estimates some people have formed of this treasure.
elegant: The bank's total reserves have long been a matter of speculation. With roughly 2,000 account holders averaging an estimated 1,500 pounds sterling each, the total would be about 3 million pounds (33 million guilders) -- a substantial sum for commerce, but far below the extravagant figures commonly imagined.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam's total reserves, estimated from about 2,000 accounts at roughly 1,500 pounds sterling each, likely amounted to about 3 million pounds sterling -- substantial but far below popular legend.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam's reserves were likely about 3 million pounds sterling -- substantial but far below the extravagant estimates that circulated.
notes: Smith attempts a back-of-envelope estimate of the bank's reserves. 2,000 account holders -- a surprisingly small number for Europe's premier bank. 1,500 pounds average -- Smith considers this 'very large.' 3 million pounds / 33 million guilders -- roughly comparable to the annual revenue of a medium European state. 'Vastly below the extravagant ideas' -- debunking the myth of Amsterdam's bottomless treasure. Cross-reference: 4.3.34 on the bank's revenue streams.

refashioned:
The city of Amsterdam derives considerable revenue from the bank. Besides the warehouse rent already mentioned, each person pays a fee of ten guilders upon first opening an account, and three guilders three stivers for every new account thereafter. Every transfer costs two stivers; transfers under 300 guilders cost six stivers, to discourage small transactions. Anyone who fails to balance his account twice a year forfeits twenty-five guilders. Anyone who orders a transfer exceeding his balance must pay three percent on the overdrawn amount, and his order is voided besides.

The bank is also believed to profit considerably from selling foreign coin or bullion that falls to it through expired receipts, which it keeps until it can be sold advantageously. It profits as well from buying bank money at four percent agio and selling it at five percent. These various revenues amount to a good deal more than is needed to pay staff salaries and management expenses. The revenue from bullion storage fees alone is estimated to yield a net annual income of between 150,000 and 200,000 guilders.

Public utility, however, not revenue, was the institution's original purpose -- to relieve merchants of the disadvantages of an unfavorable exchange rate. The revenue that arose from it was unforeseen and may be considered accidental.

But it is now time to return from this long digression, into which I have been drawn while trying to explain why the exchange between countries that pay in bank money and those that pay in common currency should generally appear to favor the former and work against the latter. The former pay in a kind of money whose intrinsic value is always the same and exactly matches the standard of their respective mints; the latter pay in money whose intrinsic value is continually varying and almost always falls below that standard.
elegant:
Amsterdam derives substantial revenue from the bank: account fees, transfer charges, penalties for overdrafts and unbalanced accounts, profits from expired receipts, and the 1% spread between buying and selling bank money. Storage fees alone yield 150,000-200,000 guilders annually. Yet revenue was not the original purpose -- the bank was created for public utility, to solve the exchange problem. Its profits were accidental.

This digression explained why exchange rates appear to favor bank-money countries: they pay in money of fixed intrinsic value, while common-currency countries pay in money of fluctuating and generally degraded value.
summary: The Bank of Amsterdam generated substantial revenue through fees, penalties, expired receipts, and its bid-ask spread -- though its founding purpose was public utility, not profit. The digression explains why exchange rates systematically favor bank-money countries: they pay in standardized money while others pay in degraded currency.
distilled: The Bank of Amsterdam accidentally became a revenue source through its fee structure and expired deposits, though it was founded solely to provide merchants with reliable exchange -- which is why bank-money countries appear favored in computed exchange rates.
notes: Comprehensive summary of the bank's business model -- fees, penalties, expired receipts, market-making spread. 150,000-200,000 guilders annual net revenue from storage alone. The public utility vs. revenue distinction -- the bank's profits were an unintended consequence. Closes the digression and reconnects to the main argument: bank money's fixed value vs. common currency's variable value explains the exchange rate appearance. Cross-reference: 4.3.13 where the digression began; 4.3.35 where Smith returns to the balance-of-trade critique.
